tagged_story_for_tts:
Unpredictable storms. Stories that steady them.

Folklore comes from folk (people) + lore (knowledge passed down)—not just tales, but tools to carve order from the unknown.

When lightning splits the sky, folklore doesn’t explain the physics. It hands you a rule: "Thor’s hammer strikes—count the seconds to measure his anger." No chaos, just a rhythm. A storm isn’t random; it’s a god’s temper, and now you’ve got a job: count, predict, act. Folklore turns fear into a script.

The !Kung people of the Kalahari tell of //Gauwa, the trickster who upends the world—then fixes it. His stories aren’t morals; they’re rehearsals. When drought comes, they’ve already lived through //Gauwa’s chaos in tales. The steps? Share food, mock the trickster, endure. Folklore is a dress rehearsal for disaster.

Japanese yōkai don’t just lurk; they enforce limits. A tengu punishes arrogance by leading hikers astray—unless you bow first. Rules emerge: respect the mountain, mark trails, don’t wander at dusk. Chaos becomes a puzzle with known pieces.

Three related subjects are rituals as survival guides, how myths encode climate knowledge, and why brains crave patterns.
